findings.topic: Revenue vs profit distinction at scale
findings.title: High revenue means nothing without margin — $100k revenue can yield $3k profit
findings.claim: Agencies can hit $100,000/month in revenue while netting as little as $3,000 in profit, so raw revenue targets are misleading without tracking margin.
findings.what_they_do: TDM explicitly warns against 'brute forcing' to $50–70k/month and treating it as success; they frame margin as the true health metric, arguing that low-margin agencies cannot reinvest in better team, systems, or experimentation, which caps growth.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented internal baseline for margin targets or profit-vs-revenue tracking (§7 GAP). Weekly invoices are auto-computed but there is no documented P&L framework or margin floor that triggers a scaling decision.
findings.recommendation: Define a minimum margin % threshold (e.g., 30–40% net) as an internal KPI alongside gross revenue. Build a simple P&L view into the admin portal or Airtable so Josh and Evan can see whether revenue growth is actually profitable before committing to new hires or infrastructure.

sources.quote: some agencies will do $100,000 in revenue and $3,000 in profit

findings.title: Take a week off at $100k/month to expose founder dependency before it becomes a ceiling
findings.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
findings.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.
findings.vs_us: Palm is early-stage with Josh leading acquisition and strategy hands-on (§6). No documented test for founder dependency exists. Given the current scale, this is a forward-looking tool, but the diagnostic framework is immediately useful to plan against.
findings.recommendation: Schedule a planned 5-day founder-absence drill (even a partial one — Josh steps back from one department at a time) to surface which processes break. Use the results to prioritize which workflows need SOPs or a designated owner first. Document the gaps in Airtable.

sources.quote: take a week off and just observe what happens

findings.title: Convert founder gut-reactions into SOPs so the team executes your standards without you
findings.claim: Every time the founder instinctively knows how they would handle a situation, that instinct should be written into an SOP so the team follows the same standard without requiring the founder's involvement.
findings.what_they_do: TDM's framework: 'your intuition, the way you feel, should become the rules of the business.' If the founder would handle a creator issue a certain way, that becomes a documented framework the team is trained on. The goal is to remove the founder from execution while preserving their operating philosophy.
findings.vs_us: Palm's systems are strong on the content pipeline (§1) but thin on creator-facing ops, chatting protocols, and acquisition handling (§6, §8). The SOP layer for creator management and issue resolution is not documented internally.
findings.recommendation: Run a structured 'intuition capture' session: Josh walks through the 10 most common creator/fan/operational situations he handles and dictates how he'd resolve each. Evan documents these as v1 SOPs in Airtable or Notion. Prioritize creator onboarding issues, escalation handling, and any workflow currently bottlenecked on Josh.

sources.quote: if you feel that, oh, I would deal with a creator's issue like this, then that needs to become an SOP

findings.title: Tie each team role's compensation to the specific business metric that role drives
findings.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
findings.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.
findings.vs_us: Palm's team structure is thin (§10): Amin is a contractor, editors exist, and an external chat team is outsourced. No documented incentive structure or performance-linked comp bands exist. This is a GAP as Palm begins building in-house teams.
findings.recommendation: When hiring the first in-house chatters (§8 roadmap) and any additional editors or marketing staff, design role-specific KPIs with a variable comp component tied to those KPIs (e.g., chatters earn a bonus % of PPV revenue above a baseline; editors earn a bonus per post that hits a reach threshold). Document comp bands in the org structure build-out.

sources.quote: media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.

findings.title: Use 'what breaks if revenue doubles tomorrow' as the primary systems-gap diagnostic
findings.claim: The most practical way to identify scaling bottlenecks is to ask 'what would break if our revenue doubled overnight' rather than asking 'how do we grow.'
findings.what_they_do: TDM frames the scaling question as a stress-test thought experiment: list every department and workflow, then identify which ones would snap under 2x load. The output is a prioritized list of systems, hires, and automations to build before growth demands them, not after.
findings.vs_us: Palm is in an active SMM hub redesign (§11) and has several in-flight initiatives. There is no documented stress-test exercise or pre-mortem for scaling. Given that Palm is building infrastructure now, this lens is immediately applicable.
findings.recommendation: Conduct a 'double revenue' war-game session: map every current operational workflow and ask what would break at 2x creator count or 2x content volume. Use the output to sequence the remaining build plan — prioritize fixes to workflows that would break first (likely: content pipeline throughput, Amin/posting bottleneck, creator onboarding, and the chat team handoff).

sources.quote: what would happen and what would break if revenue doubled tomorrow

findings.topic: Incremental hiring in step with revenue milestones
findings.title: Add team members and build systems in proportion to revenue growth — not all at once
findings.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
findings.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.
findings.vs_us: Palm's hiring and org structure are thin and undocumented (§10 GAP). There is no scaling roadmap that ties headcount or system builds to specific revenue milestones. Palm risks the 'brute force' trap the video warns against if growth outpaces internal structure.
findings.recommendation: Create a simple revenue-milestone roadmap in Airtable or Notion: at each $X increment in monthly revenue, define the next hire, SOP, or system to be built. Even a rough version (e.g., at $X add a second editor; at $Y formalize creator KPI reporting; at $Z begin in-house chat hiring) provides the incremental discipline TDM describes.

sources.quote: 10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.

findings.title: Offload non-core departments to third parties (accounting, traffic, chat) to buy back founder time before hiring in-house
findings.claim: Before a founder can afford or justify full-time hires in every department, outsourcing specific functions (bookkeeping, chat, traffic/affiliate) to third parties is the correct intermediate step to relieve calendar pressure and sustain growth.
findings.what_they_do: TDM explicitly names three early-stage offload candidates: an accountant for bookkeeping, a chat team for manning OF accounts, and an affiliate partner for traffic. The logic is that buying back founder time via third parties is cheaper and faster than building in-house teams prematurely.
findings.vs_us: Palm already follows this pattern for chatting (external chat team, §8) and manual posting (Amin contractor, §5). This validates the current outsourcing approach. The gap is traffic/distribution (§5 GAP) — Palm has no documented traffic affiliate or third-party traffic partner.
findings.recommendation: Validate the existing outsourcing choices (chat team, Amin) as correct for the current stage per TDM's framework. As the next offload step, explore an affiliate or third-party traffic partner for Reddit/X/Snapchat distribution (§5 GAP), rather than building in-house traffic capability first. This directly mirrors TDM's recommended sequencing.

sources.quote: working with somebody on an affiliate basis to help handle your traffic... bringing a chat team in to help you with actually manning the accounts

findings.topic: Content repurposing — structural uniqueness to evade IG detection
findings.title: Make repurposed Reels structurally different, not just color-adjusted
findings.claim: Changing only brightness/contrast is insufficient to fool Instagram's duplicate-detection; the video's structure must be genuinely altered.
findings.what_they_do: They download the original Reel watermark-free (SnapTick/SnapInsta), then apply structural edits in any editing app: trim 0.1s from a scene cut, mirror the clip, insert a different opening scene, and also adjust color/contrast slightly — the goal is a video that IS different, not merely looks different.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces organic, creator-filmed Reels and does not currently have a repurposing workflow. The structural-edit technique is a distinct add-on to the existing pipeline for any scaled reposting layer.
findings.recommendation: If Palm ever runs multi-account reposting for a single creator, document a mandatory structural-edit checklist (scene trim, mirror, new intro scene + minor color grade) before export. Add this as a step in the existing pipeline for repurposed variants, distinct from the organic primary post.

sources.quote: you're going to take 0.1 second out of one scene. You're going to basically mirror the video, right? You're going to add a different scene at the beginning

findings.topic: Multi-account setup — preventing Instagram account linkage
findings.title: Differentiate every signal across repurposing accounts to prevent IG cluster-ban
findings.claim: Instagram actively clusters accounts that share profile pictures, sounds, bios, links, and feed content, then bans them as a group.
findings.what_they_do: Repurposing accounts must have: completely different usernames (not sequential variations), different profile pictures, different feed posts, different bios, and different funneling links (Beacons vs Linktree vs direct, etc.). The only common element is the creator's face in video content.
findings.vs_us: Palm's AI-account warmup system (§3/§4) already enforces hardware and SIM isolation per persona, and uses dedicated aged Gmail accounts. However, Palm's documented warmup is for AI personas, not for multi-account real-creator repurposing. The same differentiation logic should apply if Palm ever runs repurposing account clusters for real creators.
findings.recommendation: If Palm builds a repurposing layer for real creators, extend the existing AI-account differentiation playbook (§3/§4) to cover: username diversity rules, bio templates that vary per account, and link rotation (alternate between Beacons, Linktree, direct OF link). No sequential naming conventions.

sources.quote: Different profile pictures, different usernames… different feed posts, different bio, maybe a different funneling setup

findings.topic: Multi-account content rotation — avoiding identical Reels grids
findings.title: Rotate which repurposed video goes to which account — never mirror the same grid
findings.claim: Posting identical video sequences across all repurposing accounts is the most common way agencies trigger cluster-bans.
findings.what_they_do: If a creator films 3 videos/day, all 3 go to the main organic account, but each repurposing account receives only ONE of the three — a different one per account. This ensures no two reposting accounts share the same Reels grid sequence.
findings.vs_us: Palm's current scheduling system (§5) routes posts via Publer or Telegram/Amin but is designed for one primary account per creator. There is no documented logic for distributing content across a cluster of repurposing accounts for a single creator.
findings.recommendation: Design a distribution-rotation rule in the scheduling layer: for any creator running N repurposing accounts, assign each daily video to a unique subset of those accounts via a round-robin or modular assignment in Airtable/Publer. This prevents grid mirroring and reduces ban risk.

sources.quote: if you have three different videos per day… you're going to post all three of them on the main account. One of them on the first reposting account, one of them on the second, and one of them on the third

findings.topic: Organic vs. repurposed account architecture — strict separation
findings.title: Never mix organic and repurposed content on the same IG account
findings.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
findings.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.
findings.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) is entirely organic/creator-filmed for the primary account. Palm does not currently run a repurposing-account layer. This finding clarifies the correct architecture IF Palm adds one: keep the existing organic pipeline untouched and stand up a separate repurposing operation alongside it.
findings.recommendation: If Palm scales to multi-account reposting, codify the two-tier rule in the SMM hub redesign (§11): tag accounts as 'organic-primary' or 'repurpose-only' in Airtable, and enforce via posting-workflow routing so editors never schedule repurposed content to an organic-primary account.

sources.quote: you split up into both teams because you have your main chick and you have your side chicks… Never mix the two. Never.

findings.topic: Sequencing quality before volume — avoiding premature scale
findings.title: Achieve a proven content formula before scaling repurposing volume
findings.claim: Reposting low-performing content at scale wastes operational overhead with negligible subscriber return and delays the discovery of a working content formula.
findings.what_they_do: They establish a minimum quality floor first — in their case, videos that reliably hit 50K+ views each — before launching any repurposing infrastructure. Until that floor is hit, all effort goes into improving content quality (hooks, formats, etc.). Only after consistent organic performance do they add repurposing accounts and VA infrastructure.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven and inspo-based, which is ahead of ad-hoc agencies on quality signal. However, Palm has no documented quality-gate threshold (e.g., a minimum average view count) that must be cleared before activating a repurposing or volume-scaling layer (§1 GAP — cadence/performance benchmarks not codified).
findings.recommendation: Define and document a performance gate for each creator: e.g., 'average Reel view count ≥ X over the last 10 posts before activating multi-account repurposing.' Capture this threshold in Airtable per creator and surface it in the SMM Overview dashboard (§11 in-flight).

sources.quote: before you go into the volume, you have to go into the quality… optimize the content until you have a winning formula

findings.topic: VA-staffed repurposing operation — scaling social output without creator time
findings.title: Staff a separate VA team to run repurposing accounts, freeing creators for original filming
findings.claim: Repurposing accounts are an operational task fully separable from the creator, enabling unlimited horizontal account scaling via VAs without increasing creator workload.
findings.what_they_do: Once repurposing accounts are set up (distinct identities, rotation rules), the agency hires VAs whose sole job is to download, structurally re-edit, and post content to the repurposing account cluster. The creator only films for the organic primary accounts. This decouples content volume from creator availability.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently uses Amin (contractor) for manual posting (§5) and editors for production (§1). There is no VA layer for repurposing account management. Hiring/delegation infrastructure for this role does not exist yet (§10 GAP).
findings.recommendation: When Palm is ready to scale social distribution, define a 'Repurposing VA' role with a clear SOP: download source, apply structural edits per checklist, assign to rotation slot, schedule via Publer. Start with one VA per 5 repurposing accounts. Document the SOP before hiring.

sources.quote: you can still have your main accounts, your main organic accounts, pull all the views, but you can hire 50 VAs and repost on as many accounts as you like on this side

findings.title: Target mid-tier creators (10K followers, ~$1-3K/mo) not top earners
findings.claim: Beginner agencies should target creators with ~10-15K followers already making $1-3K/month rather than chasing large verified accounts who won't respond.
findings.what_they_do: Explicitly filter outreach to creators in the 10K-15K follower range earning roughly $1-3K/month on OF, on the basis that they are more open to support, more reachable via DM, and more likely to convert to a managed relationship.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented targeting criteria (§6 GAP). No internal baseline exists for follower count, current earnings, or engagement thresholds that qualify a creator for outreach.
findings.recommendation: Codify a minimum viable creator profile for outreach: e.g., 10K-20K IG followers, $1-3K/mo existing OF revenue, active posting cadence. Add these as qualification fields to the creator onboarding form or a pre-onboarding screening step in Airtable.

sources.quote: targeting more beginner friendly creators as well. Also, someone that's kind of starting out, 10,000 15,000 followers, ones that are also making around 10K a month or so

findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+
findings.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
findings.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
findings.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

findings.title: Hard floor of 50/50 revenue split — never go lower
findings.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
findings.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.
findings.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale, floor, or tier model (§7 GAP). The commission field exists in the Creators table but the logic behind what percentage to set is unwritten.
findings.recommendation: Document Palm's commission policy explicitly: establish a floor (50% is industry-standard per this and other sources), define whether Palm ever takes less (e.g., for a high-revenue creator where 50% would be disproportionate) and codify the rationale. Add this to the creator onboarding wizard as a displayed policy, not just a blank field.

sources.quote: don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.

findings.title: Start organic on Twitter, TikTok, IG only — add Reddit/bots later
findings.claim: New agencies should limit traffic sources to organic short-form content on Twitter, TikTok, and Instagram, deferring Reddit, bots, and shoutouts until the funnel is proven.
findings.what_they_do: Recommends a three-platform organic-only traffic strategy for beginners: push short-form video content on Twitter/X, TikTok, and Instagram. Explicitly deprioritizes Reddit, paid shoutouts, and automation tools as advanced tactics — not because they don't work, but because complexity at the start obscures what's actually driving results.
findings.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is not documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG and social content production (§1) but has no written playbook for multi-platform distribution sequencing or when to layer in Reddit/paid channels.
findings.recommendation: For new creator onboarding, define a Phase 1 traffic stack (IG + TikTok organic, consistent with Palm's existing content pipeline) and a Phase 2 expansion trigger (e.g., once a creator hits X subs or Y MRR, add Reddit and X/Twitter). Document this as a traffic phase ladder so operators know when and how to escalate.

sources.quote: start simple things like Twitter, Tik Tok, Instagram, and that's it. Just push organic short form content. No need to over complicate it

findings.title: Never mention OnlyFans by name on social — use clean indirect CTAs
findings.claim: Directly referencing OnlyFans on social media platforms (IG, TikTok) risks shadowbans or account termination, so all CTAs must be indirect and discreet.
findings.what_they_do: Instructs operators to keep the social-to-OF funnel link-based but never platform-name the destination in captions, bios phrasing, or spoken content. No five-step click chains either — the funnel is: social content → link → OF page, with the OF brand name omitted from the social layer.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces social posts but has no documented compliance rules around OF mention avoidance or shadowban risk on IG/TikTok. This is a platform-risk gap (§ platform-risk) that could silently harm creator accounts.
findings.recommendation: Add a compliance rule to Palm's content/posting SOP: no direct OF platform name mentions in captions, audio, or on-screen text on IG or TikTok. Standardize CTA language (e.g., 'link in bio,' 'exclusive content,' 'join my page') across all creator accounts. Include this as a checklist item in the editor approval workflow.

sources.quote: do not mention only fans directly on social media because you are going to get banned. Shadowbanned or worse, right? Just don't do it.

findings.title: Agency founder must chatter personally before hiring chatters
findings.claim: Before delegating DM monetization to hired chatters, the agency operator must handle messaging themselves to understand fan psychology and what drives spending.
findings.what_they_do: Requires the founder to personally reply to DMs in the early stage — not as a permanent task but as a learning phase. The stated goal is to internalize why fans message, why they send money, and what emotional triggers drive PPV conversions, before writing SOPs or hiring people to do it.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal SOPs, scripts, or fan psychology documentation. This means Palm's leadership may lack the ground-level insight needed to evaluate chatter quality or build in-house scripts for the roadmap in-house chat team.
findings.recommendation: As Palm moves toward an in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap, §8), leadership should run a time-boxed hands-on chatting sprint (e.g., 2-4 weeks on 1-2 accounts) to build firsthand understanding of fan psychology before writing scripts or hiring. Document findings as the foundation of Palm's internal chatting SOP.

sources.quote: You need to understand the psychology. Why is this dude, you know, typing a message this girl online? Why is he here? Why is he sending money? You need to understand these things.

findings.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue
findings.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
findings.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) so this loop is bypassed at the current stage. However, Palm has no documented chatter comp model, hiring criteria, or onboarding process for when it builds an in-house team. This is a build-toward gap.
findings.recommendation: Begin documenting chatter comp benchmarks now (flat rate ranges, commission % norms, hybrid structures) sourced from competitor content like this. Build a chatter job description and scoring rubric ahead of the in-house chat team launch. This prevents scrambling when the timeline moves up.

sources.quote: you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.
